transcript:
Here's a burst of dots that never quite decides to be a firework or a fog — it just keeps churning, drifting slowly leftward as five invisible gravity wells fight over the same particles.

Look at what happens in the update well state function: each well gathers "accumulated mass" from nearby particles, and once that crosses its "buckling point," the well flips into a recovering state and pushes everything back out — not violently, but as a release. That's the crystallize-or-scatter coin flip happening constantly, well by well, never all at once. And notice each particle carries a "locked fraction" — it can inch toward 0.8 as it orbits close to a well, glowing warmer the more locked it gets, but the code caps it there and decays it every frame. It's built to never finish becoming.

So what you're watching isn't a system settling down — it's a system whose only stable state is almost-settling. Every bright cluster is a near-miss, not an arrival.
